name: segregation-tracking
title: Segregation Tracking Project
description: Comprehensive tracking of segregation across every U.S. neighborhood and school, from the Segregation Tracking Project (USC/Stanford). Includes the publicly available exposure-segregation dataset from Nilforoshan et al. (Nature, 2023) — socioeconomic segregation measured via anonymized mobility data for 382 metropolitan areas and 2,828 counties. The full Segregation Tracking Project dataset (racial/economic segregation in schools and neighborhoods, 1970–2020+) is available at edopportunity.org under a data use agreement.

licenses.name: other
licenses.title: Segregation Tracking Project Data Use Agreement (non-commercial, attribution required)

sources.name: Nilforoshan et al. 2023
sources.title: Nilforoshan, H. et al. Human mobility networks reveal increased segregation in large cities. Nature 623, 71–77 (2023)

sources.name: Stanford Exposure Segregation Data
sources.title: Stanford SNAP Lab — Exposure Segregation Data (county and MSA level)

resources.name: exposure-segregation-county
resources.title: Socioeconomic Exposure Segregation by County
resources.description: Socioeconomic exposure segregation index for 2,828 U.S. counties, derived from anonymized cell phone mobility data (SafeGraph, 2017). Measures the degree to which lower- and higher-income residents encounter each other in their daily movements.

fields.name: exposure_segregation
fields.description: Raw exposure segregation index. Higher values indicate greater socioeconomic segregation in daily mobility patterns.

fields.name: fips_code
fields.description: County FIPS code (5-digit Federal Information Processing Standard)

fields.name: exposure_segregation_smoothed
fields.description: Smoothed exposure segregation index (0–1 scale). Values smoothed to account for small-area estimation noise.

resources.name: exposure-segregation-msa
resources.title: Socioeconomic Exposure Segregation by Metropolitan Area
resources.description: Socioeconomic exposure segregation for 382 U.S. metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs), with bridging index. Derived from 1.6 billion person-to-person encounters among 9.6 million individuals (SafeGraph mobility data, 2017). Key finding: exposure segregation is 67% higher in the ten largest metros than in small MSAs.

fields.name: exposure_segregation
fields.description: Exposure segregation index. Higher values indicate greater socioeconomic segregation in daily mobility patterns.

fields.name: msa
fields.description: Metropolitan Statistical Area name (e.g. 'New York, NY')

fields.name: bridging_index
fields.description: Bridging index (0–1). Measures cross-socioeconomic connectivity — how much lower-income residents encounter higher-income residents relative to a random baseline. Higher values indicate more bridging across economic groups.

views.name: county-segregation-histogram
views.title: Distribution of Exposure Segregation Across U.S. Counties

views.name: msa-segregation-vs-bridging
views.title: Exposure Segregation vs. Bridging Index by Metro Area
